title: podman containers not manageable from systemd
body:
/kind bug

**Description**

containers managed by systemd start and fail after a few seconds.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a systemd unit file for HAproxy container in /etc/systemd/system/haproxy.service:

```
[Unit]
Description=haproxy container
[Service]
Restart=always
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman start haproxy
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop -t 10 haproxy
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

2. Enable the service:
```systemctl enable haproxy.service```

3. Start the service:
```systemctl start haproxy.service```

**Describe the results you received:**

```
[root@undercloud ~]# service haproxy status -l
Redirecting to /bin/systemctl status  -l haproxy.service
● haproxy.service - haproxy container
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/haproxy.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: failed (Result: start-limit) since Tue 2018-09-11 21:33:07 UTC; 2min 30s ago
  Process: 51209 ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop -t 10 haproxy (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
  Process: 51125 ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman start haproxy (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)
 Main PID: 51125 (code=exited, status=0/SUCCESS)

Sep 11 21:33:07 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: haproxy.service holdoff time over, scheduling restart.
Sep 11 21:33:07 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: start request repeated too quickly for haproxy.service
Sep 11 21:33:07 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: Failed to start haproxy container.
Sep 11 21:33:07 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: Unit haproxy.service entered failed state.
Sep 11 21:33:07 undercloud.localdomain systemd[1]: haproxy.service failed.
```

Running ``podman ps`` hangs for 5 seconds and then returns empty (no container running).

**Describe the results you expected:**

Service in systemd should be active and running.
Podman should return the list of running containers.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Output of ps:
```
root       48327  0.0  0.0  85888   892 ?        Ssl  21:32   0:00 /usr/libexec/podman/conmon -s -c 2d7308674890d95c9f3b875046cc77082c83db9fd1fc8966d5f18e30344a3c50 -u 2d7308674890d95c9f3b875046cc77082c83db9fd1fc8966d5f18e30344a3c50 -r /usr/bin/runc -b /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/2d7308674890d95c9f3b875046cc77082c83db9fd1fc8966d5f18e30344a3c50/userdata -p /var/run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/2d7308674890d95c9f3b875046cc77082c83db9fd1fc8966d5f18e30344a3c50/userdata/pidfile -l /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/2d7308674890d95c9f3b875046cc77082c83db9fd1fc8966d5f18e30344a3c50/userdata/ctr.log --exit-dir /var/run/libpod/exits --socket-dir-path /var/run/libpod/socket --log-level error
root       62322  0.0  0.0  85888   840 ?        Ssl  21:37   0:00 /usr/libexec/podman/conmon -s -c 8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80 -u 8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80 -r /usr/bin/runc -b /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80/userdata -p /var/run/containers/storage/overlay-containers/8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80/userdata/pidfile -l /var/lib/containers/storage/overlay-containers/8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80/userdata/ctr.log --exit-dir /var/run/libpod/exits --exit-command /usr/bin/podman --exit-command-arg --root --exit-command-arg /var/lib/containers/storage --exit-command-arg --runroot --exit-command-arg /var/run/containers/storage --exit-command-arg --log-level --exit-command-arg error --exit-command-arg --cgroup-manager --exit-command-arg systemd --exit-command-arg --tmpdir --exit-command-arg /var/run/libpod --exit-command-arg --storage-driver --exit-command-arg overlay --exit-command-arg container --exit-command-arg cleanup --exit-command-arg 8dc22a2955b24aac7305cd25e68c45d4e2b3645ec84a79726f55349eba51fb80 --socket-dir-path /var/run/libpod/socket --log-level error
root       62357  0.2  0.2 456408 16996 ?        Ssl  21:37   0:00 /usr/bin/podman stop -t 10 memcached
```

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 0.9.2-dev
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
host:
  Conmon:
    package: podman-0.8.5-3.gitdc5a711.el7.x86_64
    path: /usr/libexec/podman/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 1.11.2, commit: 2d86a71a1e3f3ccf65f6fb610558e660f5830fe4-dirty'

    version: 'runc version spec: 1.0.0'
  SwapFree: 1610338304
  SwapTotal: 1610608640
  arch: amd64
  cpus: 4
  hostname: undercloud.localdomain
  kernel: 3.10.0-862.11.6.el7.x86_64
  os: linux
  uptime: 36m 45.47s

  registries: []

  - docker.io
  - registry.centos.org

    number: 28
  GraphDriverName: overlay

  GraphStatus:
    Backing Filesystem: xfs
    Native Overlay Diff: "true"
    Supports d_type: "true"
  ImageStore:
    number: 29
  RunRoot: /var/run/containers/storage
```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**
Running my lab in a VM managed by libvirt.

comments.body: I briefly tried with ``podman start -a`` option, and it seems to help. I'm redeploying my lab and see if I still hit this bug.

So I managed to go further in my deployment of ~30 containers managed by systemd, by adding -a option to podman start.
It's still failing at my step 3/5 of the deployment, so I'll keep this issue open until I'm sure it wasn't on podman side.

My thinking is this is mostly a docs issue - running with `-a` causes podman to stay alive until the container dies, which is suitable for a systemd simple unit. If we wanted to just have `podman start` without -a we would need to have a forking unit with a pidfile so systemd can keep track of the container. We need to document how to properly build unitfiles somewhere - blogpost and manpages, maybe?

I haven't reached the point where all OpenStack containers are running under Podman managed by systemd, I only get one third of them. Once I'm done with it, I would be happy to submit a PR with doc + manpages update. For now my unit files look like this:

```
[Unit]
Description=haproxy container
[Service]
Restart=always
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman start -a haproxy
ExecStop=/usr/bin/podman stop -t 10 haproxy
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

I'll update this issue once I'm done with the remaining containers.
Great work on this.  I really look forward to the Blog.
@mheon @rhatdan I would be happy to write a blog post on https://www.projectatomic.io/blog if it's ok for you. However I don't have access, but I can draft it somewhere.

Thanks @EmilienM!  Actually we'll probably target that for podman.io which we're in the process of moving to atm.  (https://github.com/containers/podman.io)  I think, but am not sure you can create a blog entry under the _posts directory there.  Fair warning, we're still working out the bumps there.
@TomSweeneyRedHat ok I'll submit a PR asap this week, if it's fine for you all.
That would be awesome @EmilienM .  As an FYI, we're hoping to announce the site more widely early next week, so that would work well for us.
ok perfect. I'm at a OpenStack conference this week but I'll try to send the PR asap even today maybe.

podman does not have to be alive for the containers to run.  All containers are monitored by conmon.
So there is not thing that requires you to keep podman running to keep the container running.

podman run --name DAEMON -d DAEMONIMAGE

Will launch a container and exit.

If the container exits later, conmon will record the appropriate data and cleanup.

If you attach to a container "-a", I am not sure what happens when you kill the podman process, It might signal conmon to die, since the connection to the tty died.

> If you attach to a container "-a", I am not sure what happens when you kill the podman process

that is a case I'm concerned about, thank you for clarification (we gotta test such a case)!
Best to try it out and see what happens.
